one_line_takeaway: A systematic-literature-review-based taxonomy (14 quality attributes plus design options) and a Swiss-Cheese-Model reference architecture for stacking multi-layered runtime guardrails across quality attributes, pipeline stages, and agent artifacts of FM-based agents -- conceptual guidance, not an empirically validated system.
key_finding: Existing guardrail approaches are predominantly single-layered, applied narrowly to individual artifacts (e.g., prompts or FM outputs) and focused on functional correctness while overlooking quality attributes like customizability and interpretability; if one such guardrail fails, risks bypass it. The paper argues a multi-layered, Swiss-Cheese-style arrangement across quality attributes, pipeline stages, and artifacts is needed, and operationalizes this via a target-to-action mapping (Table II) and a four-component reference architecture (external environment, agent components, built-in multi-layered guardrails, and an AgentOps observability/logging layer that can feed data back to activate relevant guardrails).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is two conceptual artifacts derived from a systematic literature review (SLR) of 32 selected studies: (1) a taxonomy of RUNTIME guardrails for foundation-model (FM) based agents, organized into 14 quality attributes and a set of design options (actions, targets, rules, applicability scope, modality, underlying models); and (2) a 'Swiss Cheese Model'-inspired reference architecture for layering multiple runtime guardrails along three dimensions -- quality attributes, pipeline stages, and agent artifacts. The Swiss Cheese framing is the central idea: each guardrail 'slice' has holes (gaps/weaknesses), but stacking layers whose holes are positioned differently yields a robust combined defense. Note: prior guardrail tools such as NeMo Guardrails, OpenAI's Moderation API, and GuardAgent are discussed in the paper's Related Work as CITED prior art, not as this paper's contribution; the authors explicitly position their gap as the absence of any framework giving comprehensive guidance on MULTI-LAYERED runtime guardrails.
threat_model: The paper frames (rather than formally specifies) a broad set of runtime risks unique to autonomous, non-deterministic FM-based agents rather than a single adversary model. Risks it catalogues from the literature include: generation of harmful/offensive content, mis/disinformation and hallucinations, data leakage of sensitive/confidential information (cites the 2023 Samsung/ChatGPT proprietary-data leak), adversarial queries that extract sensitive data, integrity/confidentiality/availability attacks (cites the Microsoft Tay chatbot manipulation), memory poisoning, poisoned/compromised narrow models, and unsafe tool invocation. The 'attacker' is thus a mix of malicious users, adversarial inputs, and emergent unsafe agent behavior. This is a genuine AI-safety/security scoping, not a forced frame, but the paper does not define a rigorous formal threat model or attacker capability set.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) the reference architecture is NOT operationally validated -- validation is explicitly deferred to future work; (2) taxonomy/architecture validation relied on expert feedback from AI/software-engineering specialists, risking bias toward experts' views; (3) standard SLR search/selection bias, and the March 2024 cutoff means later work is excluded; (4) the rapid evolution of FM-based agents may render parts of the findings outdated, requiring continual re-evaluation; (5) generalizability to specialized domains (healthcare, finance) may require adaptation. Beyond stated limits: the target-to-action mappings are described as illustrative, not exhaustive or definitional, so the taxonomy is guidance rather than a validated design method, and no effectiveness metrics accompany any recommendation.

datasets_used: No ML dataset; the study corpus is 32 high-quality selected primary studies drawn via SLR
Literature databases searched: ACM, IEEE Xplore, Springer, ScienceDirect, and Google Scholar (search executed March 2024)
quantitative_results: After removing duplicates and editorials: 1625 papers
After applying exclusion criteria: 101 papers; after applying inclusion criteria: 21 papers
Backward snowballing returned 189 papers; inclusion/exclusion removed 174, adding 15 more
Final cross-check and merge yielded 32 selected studies used to build the taxonomy

Taxonomy identifies 14 quality attributes: accuracy, efficiency, privacy, security, safety, fairness, compliance, generalizability, customizability, adaptability, traceability, portability, interoperability, interpretability
13 guardrail actions defined: block, filter, flag, modify, validate, parallel calls, retry, fall back, human intervention, defer, isolate, redundancy, evaluate
Reference architecture has 4 components and organizes guardrails along 3 dimensions (quality attributes, pipelines, artifacts)
Quality Assessment Criteria: 5 criteria scored 1-5; studies with average below 2 excluded
baselines_compared: None -- this is an SLR/taxonomy paper with no empirical experiments; no quantitative baseline comparison is performed. Prior tools (NeMo Guardrails, OpenAI Moderation API, GuardAgent) are discussed qualitatively as related work, not benchmarked.
